text: 17g of fat. saturated fat is a major pressure point. for dairy, the fat side can either add richness or drag the whole thing down.
3.1g of carbs. sugar, glycemic index, and starch are doing most of the work. for dairy, the question is whether the payoff is worth the baggage.
28g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. useful protein can rescue a lot of weaker traits here.
vitamin b12 at 28% daily value, vitamin a at 12% daily value stand out most. that is only mild support overall.
calcium at 51% daily value, zinc at 19% daily value, magnesium at 5% daily value stand out most. useful, but still not a major mineral edge.

the upsides first: easy protein delivery for normal diets because dairy gets real credit for convenience. clear calcium identity because that is a core category strength. slots into meals very easily because practical versatility matters a lot for dairy.

on the weaker side: not everyone tolerates dairy well because that is a real use-case limitation. fat quality varies wildly across dairy because some dairy is clean, some really is not. processing changes the category a lot because the label alone does not tell the whole story.
B tier. Best when the protein payoff or fermentation angle outweighs the fat and sodium tradeoffs.
